title: MLflow
definition: An open-source MLOps platform with four components: Tracking (log experiments, metrics, parameters), Projects (package code for reproducibility), Models (standard model format), and Registry (manage model versions and lifecycle).
why_used: The most widely used open-source experiment tracking tool. Lets you compare runs, reproduce experiments, and promote models to production through a registered model lifecycle.
interview_point: MLflow Tracking automatically logs: parameters (hyperparameters), metrics (loss, accuracy over time), artifacts (plots, model files), and tags (metadata). The Model Registry manages model stages: None → Staging → Production → Archived. mlflow.autolog() can automatically log sklearn, XGBoost, PyTorch, etc. without any manual logging code.
limitations: Storage management can be complex at scale
real_world_example:
```python
import mlflow
import mlflow.sklearn
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score

mlflow.set_tracking_uri('http://localhost:5000')  # MLflow server
mlflow.set_experiment('fraud-detection-v2')

with mlflow.start_run(run_name='rf-baseline'):
    # Log hyperparameters
    params = {'n_estimators': 200, 'max_depth': 10, 'random_state': 42}
    mlflow.log_params(params)
    
    # Train
    model = RandomForestClassifier(**params)
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    
    # Log metrics
    auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1])
    mlflow.log_metric('roc_auc', auc)
    
    # Log model with signature
    signature = mlflow.models.infer_signature(X_train, model.predict(X_train))
    mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model', signature=signature)
    
    print(f'Run ID: {mlflow.active_run().info.run_id}')
```

title: Weights & Biases
definition: A cloud-based MLOps platform for experiment tracking, dataset versioning, model visualization, and hyperparameter sweeps. Known for its superior visualization capabilities and deep learning integrations.
why_used: Best-in-class visualization for deep learning experiments. Automatically logs GPU/CPU utilization, system metrics, and media (images, audio, video). Sweeps enable sophisticated hyperparameter optimization.
interview_point: W&B's killer feature: real-time experiment visualization while training. Log images, audio, video, and custom plots directly. Sweeps support Bayesian Optimization, grid search, and random search — distributed across machines. The Artifacts system versions datasets and models with automatic lineage tracking.
limitations: Requires internet connection for cloud mode
real_world_example:
```python
import wandb
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Initialize run
wandb.init(
    project='image-classification',
    name='resnet50-exp1',
    config={
        'learning_rate': 0.001,
        'batch_size': 32,
        'epochs': 50,
        'architecture': 'ResNet50'
    }
)

# Training loop
for epoch in range(config.epochs):
    train_loss, val_loss, val_acc = train_one_epoch(model, ...)
    
    # Log metrics
    wandb.log({
        'epoch': epoch,
        'train_loss': train_loss,
        'val_loss': val_loss,
        'val_accuracy': val_acc,
        # Log sample predictions as images
        'predictions': wandb.Image(sample_image, caption=f'Pred: {pred}')
    })

wandb.finish()
```

title: DVC
definition: Git-like version control for ML data and models. Tracks large files (datasets, models) in external storage (S3, GCS, local) while storing lightweight metadata in Git. Also defines ML pipelines as DAGs of reproducible stages.
why_used: Git cannot version large datasets and model files. DVC stores data in remote storage (S3, GCS, Azure) and tracks references in Git — enabling full reproducibility of the entire ML pipeline.
interview_point: DVC uses two components: 1) Data versioning — .dvc files track large files in Git while actual data lives in remote storage. 2) Pipelines — dvc.yaml defines stages with dependencies and outputs; DVC skips unchanged stages automatically (caching). `dvc repro` reruns only changed pipeline stages.
advantages: Versions data alongside code in Git
Works with any storage (S3, GCS, local)
limitations: Not ideal for very frequent dataset updates
real_world_example:
```bash
# Initialize DVC in a git repo
git init && dvc init

# Add remote storage
dvc remote add -d myremote s3://my-bucket/dvcstore

# Track a large dataset
dvc add data/dataset.csv
git add data/dataset.csv.dvc .gitignore
git commit -m 'Add dataset v1'
dvc push  # Upload to S3

# Define pipeline in dvc.yaml
# dvc run -n preprocess \
#   -d data/raw.csv -d src/preprocess.py \
#   -o data/processed.csv \
#   python src/preprocess.py

# Reproduce pipeline (skips unchanged stages)
dvc repro

# Switch to a previous dataset version
git checkout v1.0
dvc checkout  # Downloads that version from remote
```

title: Model Drift
definition: The degradation of a deployed model's performance over time due to changes in the real-world data distribution. Two types: Data Drift (input distribution changes) and Concept Drift (relationship between input and output changes).
why_used: Understanding drift is critical for production ML — all deployed models degrade over time. Monitoring drift enables proactive retraining before significant business impact.
interview_point: Data Drift: P(X) changes — the input feature distribution shifts (e.g., seasonal trends, new user demographics). Concept Drift: P(Y|X) changes — the meaning of features changes (e.g., post-COVID 'normal' travel patterns are different). Detection methods: Population Stability Index (PSI), KS test, Chi-squared test, monitoring prediction distribution.
advantages: Detecting drift early prevents silent model failures
limitations: Requires labeled data for concept drift detection
Distinguishing drift types can be challenging
real_world_example:
```python
from evidently.report import Report
from evidently.metric_preset import DataDriftPreset, TargetDriftPreset
from evidently.metrics import ColumnDriftMetric

# Compare reference (training) data to current (production) data
report = Report(metrics=[
    DataDriftPreset(),
    TargetDriftPreset(),
    ColumnDriftMetric(column_name='age'),
])

report.run(
    reference_data=train_df,
    current_data=production_df
)

report.save_html('drift_report.html')

# Check if drift detected
result = report.as_dict()
if result['metrics'][0]['result']['dataset_drift']:
    trigger_retraining_pipeline()
```
